title: Semiclassical phases of charged spin-$1/2$ matter-wave interferometers in gravitational wave backgrounds
abstract: A matter wave propagating through curved spacetime accumulates phase that encodes both geometry and gauge structure. We develop a semiclassical framework for charged spin-$1/2$ matter-wave interferometers based on a WKB expansion of the covariant Dirac equation, in which the phase decomposes into dynamical, spin, and electromagnetic Aharonov-Bohm (AB) contributions. In a freely falling detector frame, all three channels are governed by local tidal fields. In a weak gravitational-wave (GW) background, the dynamical and spin phases probe the gravitoelectric and gravitomagnetic sectors of curvature, while the AB phase arises from curvature-induced electromagnetic fields obtained from Maxwell's equations in curved spacetime. For a Mach-Zehnder interferometer (MZI), all three responses are determined by the same tidal scale, $\ddot{h}_A \sim Ω^2_{gw}h_0$, and filtered by a common geometric kernel, while entering through distinct physical couplings. In particular, the AB contribution depends not only on the enclosed flux but also on spatial variations of the induced fields and exhibits an intrinsic frequency dependence set by the traversal time. These results provide a unified description of matter-wave interferometric phases in time-dependent GW backgrounds and identify complementary dynamical, spin, and electromagnetic pathways through which spacetime curvature imprints itself on quantum interference.
summary: This paper provides a unified theoretical description of how charged spin-1/2 matter-wave interferometers sense gravitational waves. It demonstrates that the total interferometric phase consists of three distinct channels: a dynamical phase, a spin-dependent phase, and an electromagnetic Aharonov-Bohm phase. While all three channels are driven by the same tidal curvature scale, they arise from different physical couplings—namely, inertial motion, spin-curvature coupling, and curvature-induced electromagnetic perturbations. This work identifies how spacetime curvature imprints itself on quantum interference through multiple, complementary pathways.
main_problem: Developing a unified, gauge-invariant semiclassical framework to describe how the different components of a charged spin-1/2 matter-wave interferometer phase respond to time-dependent gravitational wave backgrounds.
main_result: The study identifies three distinct, complementary phase channels (dynamical, spin, and electromagnetic AB) that all probe the same tidal curvature scale, with the AB phase specifically arising from GW-induced perturbations to the electromagnetic field.
method: A semiclassical WKB expansion of the covariant Dirac equation is used within the Fermi Normal Coordinate framework to analyze phase accumulation in a freely falling detector frame.
model_or_system: A charged spin-1/2 matter-wave interferometer (such as a Mach-Zehnder configuration) propagating through a weak gravitational wave background with an additional background magnetic field.
key_observables: Total accumulated phase difference, decomposed into dynamical phase, spin holonomy (spin phase), and electromagnetic Aharon-Bohm phase.
important_parameters: Tidal scale (ddot{h} ~ Omega^2 h0), particle mass (m), charge (q), interferometer side length (L), GW frequency (Omega_gw), and background magnetic field (B0).
main_assumptions: Semiclassical (WKB) limit, long-wavelength limit (L << lambda_gw), weak gravitational wave background, and the quasi-static approximation for tidal fields.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a derivation of the semiclassical framework from the Dirac equation, proceeds to decompose the phase into three physical channels, analyzes the electromagnetic response of the system to curvature, and concludes by comparing the scaling and sensitivity of the different phase components.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
